TextENGNew: I held my breath and chugged the beer in one go.
...The aftertaste was just as awful.
Even so, after belching out the carbonation building in my throat, I finally steeled my resolve.
First I took a deep breath, then fished a business card from the notebook in my breast pocket and held it up for everyone to see.
"All right then, I'll start with my introduction.@k@vS10/62/410100409. My name is Ryuunosuke Arakawa.@k@vS10/62/410100410. I'm a top freelance writer!"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Top... you say?"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: The kimono-clad woman let out an amused chuckle.
...Shut it. I'm running on liquid courage here, so don't call me on it.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "I specialize in the occult.@k@vS10/62/410100412. By the way, this month's Weekly Seven 'Ghost Village Special Edition' — that was written by yours truly.@k@vS10/62/410100413. Nice to meet you all."
"Ghost Village Special Edition...?@k@vS10/42/410600015. Then, you're the one who wrote that article?"
The young woman, who had seemed the least interested of anyone, unexpectedly perked up and raised her head.
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: "...You've heard of it?"
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: "I read that special edition too.@k@vS10/41/410200038. It covered all sorts of places — I thought it was pretty interesting..."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Why, thank you.@k@vS10/62/410100415. It required a lot of research, so I ran into many difficulties writing it.@k@vS10/62/410100416. But hearing your praise makes it all worthwhile."
Being praised for my work made me feel just a little proud.
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: ...But the kimono-clad woman just tilted her head skeptically, managing only a tepid 'Is that so?'
"Weekly Seven... huh~...@k@vS10/43/410400043. If everyone's reading it, it must be quite popular."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "I don't read it very often, but I always catch the headlines on the train ads.@k@vS10/42/410600019. Oh, and the psychic special edition from a few months ago was really interesting!"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Wait... you don't know the Weekly Seven?"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Never heard of it."

The Weekly Seven is a famous gossip magazine that targets a wide demographic. Since its founding in the Heisei era, it's been featured in TV commercials and everywhere else...
changeReason: Numerical values differ between translations.

TextENGNew: Not having read it was one thing, but I'd never met someone who hadn't even heard of it before.
She must be living a life utterly cut off from the outside world.
"The Weekly Seven is advertised pretty often on posters and in newspapers, so you've probably seen it before.@k@vS10/41/410200040. The logo has red text on a white background."
"Unfortunately, I can't recall seeing any advertisements like that."
The woman in the kimono looked puzzled as she said that. ...How heartbreaking.
"...I'm surprised you haven't heard of the Weekly Seven.@k@vS10/62/410100421. Do you not read magazines much?"
"...It's not that.@k@vS10/43/410400047. So, is that article the reason all of you came here?"

TextENGNew: "Oh, no...@k@vS10/41/410200042. I'm here by coincidence, actually."

changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: ...Even if it was true, a little social grace wouldn't have hurt. They could have at least said something like 'The article piqued my interest.'
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "So, Arakawa — you came here for work? Reporting or something?"
"Of course. For the occu..."
I was about to say 'Occult Special, second edition'... but stopped myself.
A group of unknown men and women gathered in an abandoned building — that was already occult enough on its own.
Not to mention they all had that dead look in their eyes...
"A journalist, huh.@k@vS10/43/410400050. ...Looking at someone like you makes me want to say: take better care of yourself."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "...Thanks for the concern."
There was something dismissive about the way she said it — as if she were belittling my profession. It didn't sit well with me.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: The kimono-clad woman popped open another cup of sake and jerked her chin toward the young woman.

TextENGNew: Caught off guard by the sudden attention, the young woman blushed slightly and gave a small bow.
"My name is Yae... Towada.@k@vS10/42/410600025. And the one sleeping over there is my companion, Takumi Kurosawa."
She turned and glanced back at the young man sleeping in the corner of the room.
He seemed to be sleeping soundly — there was no sign of him waking despite our conversation.
"Hmm... are you two married?"
"Oh, no... that's not it at all...!@k@vS10/42/410600027. We're not married!!@k@vS10/42/410600028. He's, um... he's my boyfriend."

Still, the fact that she clearly said 'boyfriend' suggested they'd been together for a while.

TextENGNew: "We drove here together.@k@vS10/42/410600030. This is the first time the two of us have traveled anywhere on our own, so I'm just a little nervous...@k@vS10/42/410600031. We came here on a one-day trip from Tokyo and Takumi-kun was really tired, so he'll probably sleep for a while longer."
"Oh, and I'm not a student... I have a job, at least for now.@k@vS10/42/410600033. Umm... It's very nice to meet you!"
Towada-san rattled it all off in one anxious breath, like an audience member suddenly called up to appear on a variety show.

TextENGNew: I wasn't entirely sure what she was asking us to be kind about, but both Otobe and I gave a small nod in return.

TextENGNew: "...Yes, so?"

TextENGNew: "No, I mean...@k@vS10/62/410100428. It just struck me as unusual — going on a date to a village where every resident died."

TextENGNew: "...Oh really?"

Well, there are plenty of couples who visit occult spots for fun, so maybe it shouldn't be that surprising...

TextENGNew: And yet, something didn't fit. She didn't look like the kind of girl who'd use ghost stories as an excuse to get drunk with a guy in a place like this.

TextENGNew: "...Well, to each their own.@k@vS10/43/410400056. But leaving his girlfriend behind while he drinks himself unconscious — not much of a gentleman, is he?@k@vS10/43/410400058. Heh heh heh."
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: The kimono-clad woman shot a pointed look at the sleeping young man as she said that.

TextENGNew: "Oh, sure..."

The shochu bottle was still more than half full. Empty beer cans were scattered about, but only one had lipstick on the rim.
Judging by the way the kimono-clad woman had been guzzling sake, I suspected Towada-san could barely drink at all.
Meanwhile, Otobe had been nervously picking at a hangnail the entire time, listening to our conversation.
"Otobe. Here, eat something."
"Huh? Oh, ok.@k@vS10/41/410200044. Is this... squid?"
"What, you don't like squid?"
"Then eat up!@k@vS10/43/410400064. That's why you look so pale — you're not eating enough."

changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "Hehehe... well, that's one thing you don't share.@k@vS10/43/410400071. You certainly don't look the type for baseball."

I seized on her words to press the question.

TextENGNew: "...I'm sorry."

Something about his mumbling, indecisive tone... irritated me.
I've never been good with grown adults who can't speak clearly... No, let me be honest — I straight-up don't like them.

TextENGNew: "...Hey, what's your name?"
That's why I couldn't help it — even knowing it was petty, my voice came out rougher than intended.

...This guy too. Honestly, it was hard to believe someone this timid had come to a place like this alone.
As I stared, Otobe quickly averted his gaze. Then he started squirming uncomfortably again.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "So you drove all the way out here too.@k@vS10/62/410100437. ...By yourself?"

"Right..."
"...You got separated? ...How?"
When I pressed him further, Otobe suddenly went quiet and lowered his head.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: ...Damn. My impatience had gotten the better of me — I'd been too harsh.

TextENGNew: Realizing this, I cleared my throat, calmed myself, and was about to try a gentler approach.

TextENGNew: Then—
"On the way here... everyone just vanished..."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: At Otobe's words, the room fell silent for a moment.
"They vanished...?@k@vS10/42/410600039. What do you mean by 'vanished'...?"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "...Whoa, are we suddenly telling ghost stories?@k@vS10/62/410100442. Save that for when things warm up a bit more--"
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "That's not what this is!@k@vS10/41/410200067. It really happened!"
Otobe slammed his fist against the tatami and turned to face me, his expression dead serious. ...His intensity made me drop the jokes and lean in.
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "...What happened exactly?@k@vS10/62/410100444. Explain it to me."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "We left Tokyo this morning... and traveled all the way here through the mountains.@k@vS10/41/410200069. A friend of mine handled the driving.@k@vS10/41/410200070. So, after a while... we came to a place with a nice view of the entire village."
For people used to city life, the scenery certainly does look picturesque. If they arrived in the afternoon when it wasn't raining, it would make sense to stop the car and take a look around.

"And while we were there, I decided to take a quick bathroom break...@k@vS10/41/410200072. I went into the woods looking for a good spot...@k@vS10/41/410200073. but when I came back... everyone was gone...@k@vS10/41/410200074. Even the car had disappeared..."
The story was so disconnected that it was hard to take at face value. Besides...
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Look... I hate to say this, but...@k@vS10/62/410100446. isn't it possible your friends just left you behind?"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

"...There's no way!"
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: Otobe slapped his dried squid down on the tatami and fixed me with a sharp glare.

TextENGNew: "...Fair enough. Sorry, sorry.@k@vS10/62/410100451. No need to get so worked up. I'm just talking possibilities."

changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: Having his friends doubted had clearly struck a nerve.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: ...Was that really something worth getting this upset over?
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: Just then—

TextENGNew: "...Hehehe."
The kimono-clad woman knocked back her third cup of sake and let out a quiet laugh.
"...What is it?"
"That sort of thing isn't a big deal."
"What... is that supposed to mean...?"
All three of us fixed our eyes on the kimono-clad woman at once.
She gave a generous, bewitching smile... and spoke.
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "None of you three are from this village, after all.@k@vS10/43/410400081. I suppose it's only natural you wouldn't know..."

TextENGNew: "It's called... 'Onikakushi.'"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: Onikakushi...
No — actually, I had heard of that.
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: If I recalled correctly... it was an ancient custom that had been passed down in Hinamizawa.
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Around here, people have always been known to vanish without a trace.@k@vS10/43/410400084. In this village, they call that 'Onikakushi.'"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "But isn't that what's usually called 'spirited away'?@k@vS10/42/410600046. Why do they call it 'Onikakushi'?"
Towada-san asked timidly. ...But the kimono-clad woman just smirked and didn't answer.
The silence was growing awkward, so I instinctively jumped in to explain.
"This village... used to be called 'Onigafuchi Village.'"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Onigafuchi..."
"...Legend has it that the bottom of the swamp the village was named after connected to the underworld where demons dwelt."

"The swamp was rumored to be deeper than the sea — a bottomless abyss where anything swallowed would sink all the way to the netherworld.@k@vS10/62/410100460. And that swamp was called 'Onigafuchi' — the Demon's Abyss."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Eh...?@k@vS10/41/410200091. So then, this name written here, Onigafuchi..."
"That's right.@k@vS10/62/410100462. The village's name was changed to "Hinamizawa" during the Meiji era.@k@vS10/62/410100463. Up until then, this village was known as "Onigafuchi" village."
At those words, Otobe looked up at the 'Onigafuchi Defense Alliance' poster stuck to the wall.
One day, demons emerged from the swamp one after another.
...Demons came overflowing from Hell.
The villagers believed as much, and they were absolutely terrified.
The demons came forth from the swamp and attacked the villagers without mercy.
The humans were powerless before the demons.
The villagers were terrified, confused, and powerless — all they could do was flee.
If things continued like that, the village would be destroyed by the demons...
Just when the villagers were about to give up hope, a god descended... 'Oyashiro-sama.'
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "This Oyashiro-sama was a compassionate deity.@k@vS10/62/410100465. The demons ceased their assault on the villagers and prostrated themselves before Oyashiro-sama's divine authority, obeying willingly."
"And then... the demons quietly returned to Hell?"

"It would have been nice if that was the case, but it seems the demons had circumstances of their own.@k@vS10/62/410100467. These demons were allegedly exiled from hell, and cried out to Oyashiro-sama that they had no place to return to."
Across both Hell and the human world, these demons had no place to call home...
And so, through Oyashiro-sama's mediation, the villagers were ultimately forced to find a way to coexist with the demons.

"Oyashiro-sama gave the demons human form so they could live alongside the villagers.@k@vS10/62/410100469. Over time, humans and demons intermarried, and the village became a community of half-human, half-demon hybrids."
I paused there, cleared my throat, and lowered my voice before continuing.
"But... the problem is what came after that."
"Problem...?"
"These weren't just any demons. They were man-eating demons."
"...?! Then that means..."
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "...Even with human form, a man-eating demon is still a demon.@k@vS10/62/410100473. Every so often, something would trigger their instinct for 'the hunt,' and they wouldn't be able to resist."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Even though their blood had been diluted through intermarriage, the demon instinct was imprinted in their DNA.@k@vS10/62/410100475. But they couldn't very well cannibalize their own villagers."
"...So they would venture down to other settlements at regular intervals and..."
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "Don't tell me... they kidnapped people...?!"
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: In other words, 'Onikakushi' — 'being demoned away' — was a ritualized human hunt carried out by the demons...!

changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "...Why, thank you."

TextENGNew: "Being abducted by demons so they can feed on you...?"

"What happened to Oyashiro-sama...?@k@vS10/42/410600056. Why didn't Oyashiro-sama step in?"

changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "On the nights when sacrifices were taken, there was apparently a ritual called the 'Watanagashi.'@k@vS10/62/410100485. 'Wata' refers to a person's innards... In other words, the sacrifice's intestines were pulled out and offered to Oyashiro-sama — that was the Watanagashi."

"It's fine, it's fine.@k@vS10/43/410400095. Well, it can't be helped.@k@vS10/43/410400096. That's the situation we're in. It'd be stranger if you weren't afraid.@k@vS10/43/410400097. ...After all, even if they're not nearby, there are plenty of people who've died in this very village."

TextENGNew: This woman... is she really human...?

TextENGNew: Just then — Sonozaki-san suddenly stopped in her tracks.

TextENGNew: "Got it? Don't make a sound."

"Hey you, can you hear anything...?"
"...Absolutely nothing."
I was about to smile with relief at Otobe's answer... when I heard it, clearly.
*crunch*... *crunch*... *crunch*...

TextENGNew: So how could she possibly lose her bearings now...?

Just being allowed to see the inside was a windfall. I'd commit every detail to memory and put it in an article...

I was so worked up I could barely form coherent words.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

I jumped away from the door.
Sonozaki-san raised the katana and placed its tip precisely against the padlock.

Sonozaki-san closed her eyes... then snapped them open. And—
*CLANG*--! ...*clatter*...
...I couldn't see it very well.
When I came to my senses, Sonozaki-san's sword was pointing downward... and the padlock, split cleanly in two, lay on the ground.

TextENGNew: Sonozaki-san strode straight through our dumbstruck little group without breaking stride.

changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: What on earth is this woman...?!

Mixed with the sharp tang of mold and dust was a metallic smell — like iron. And then—
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "It's pitch dark, so take care not to fall."
"Ah, alright. ...Oh."
Otobe was shining his flashlight around, then stopped when he found a circuit breaker attached to the wall, and reached out to it.
I looked up at the bare light bulbs hanging from the ceiling, but they were either burnt out or dead — no light came on.
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: "...The flashlights are more than enough.@k@vS10/43/410400153. In fact, it's more convenient that way."
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: Stepping in a little farther, we found another set of doors.
Sonozaki-san put her hands against the door and pushed... hard... harder...
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "Urgh..."
Just watching how she strained told me how impossibly heavy those doors were.
Otobe and I moved in to help, pressing our weight against the door alongside her. ...Eventually, with a grinding creak, the doors gave way.
changeReason: Complete rewrite with substantially different content; may indicate misaligned translation.

TextENGNew: "Whoa...!"
Whoa... whoa...
My voice echoed.
Before us stretched a space far larger than I had imagined.
changeReason: Substantially reworded with different semantic content that could alter reader understanding.

TextENGNew: And simultaneously... a foul, acrid stench hit my nostrils. The air was thick and murky, like rotting fish. And then—
changeReason: At least one sentence completely rewritten with different content.

TextENGNew: "This thing right in front of us... is that Oyashiro-sama...?"
Deep in the back of the ritual storehouse, a divine figure stood — something like a Buddhist statue.
"This is the guardian god of Hinamizawa?@k@vS10/41/410200136. It's amazing..."
Taking a look at my surroundings, the walls, ceilings, shelves, everything were filled with various chisels and carpentry tools.
Looking a little closer, I noticed a steel cage hanging from the ceiling... and some kind of restraining device in the corner of the room.
And on the table, there were several chain restraint mechanisms.
"This... is where the festival tools are supposed to be stored, right...?@k@vS10/41/410200138. But... isn't it a little strange?"
"...A natural question.@k@vS10/62/410100556. Though the "Watanagashi" festival was always a little strange to begin with..."
When Otobe asked that in a trembling voice, I responded as such and turned my gaze toward the carving knives, saws, and other large slicing tools hanging on the wall.

"The festival of Hinamizawa... is said to have been the festival where the demons ate humans.@k@vS10/62/410100558. Watanagashi... with "wata" meaning organs.@k@vS10/62/410100559. It's a ritualistic ceremony of sending entrails drifting downstream."
...That's right. These aren't mere tools.
They were actually used... in service of spilling human blood.
"These are... those tools...@k@vS10/40/410300405. I can't believe something like that truly existed..."
Miyuki-chan examined each and every tool with sharp eyes.
Unlike Otobe, whose eyes wandered all around, trembling.
"So, all the tools in here were for killing people...@k@vS10/40/410300407. In the folk tale, the humans and demons were able to coexist... but I guess this means reality wasn't so sweet?"
"...Miyuki-chan."
Miyuki-chan said some frightening things in such a nonchalant tone of voice.
While feeling surprised by Miyuki-chan's courage, I took another look at the tools.
I could picture it clearly in my head: A human sacrifice enduring absolute torment while hanging on the border of life and death.
I couldn't see a rational need for these tools to be created.
But... if these tools were used to butcher the sacrifice while they were still alive and prepare them for consumption, the elaborate designs and decorations all pointed to a single purpose.
In other words, the murders were carried out before an audience.
And even the villagers must've found enjoyment in such a cruel sight...
That thought... made me start feeling nauseous.
"Ryuunosuke-san, are you all right...?"
"Ah... yeah. I'm fine..."
"Hehehe.@k@vS10/43/410400156. Looks like you're totally cowering in fear."
Miyuki-chan put a hand on my shoulder to try and comfort me, but I just raised my hands and awkwardly laughed it off.
After watching that, Sonozaki-san turned around and continued searching for what she was looking for in the dimly lit room.
"This is the reality of being demoned away.@k@vS10/43/410400158. Otobe, perhaps your friends are being butchered and eaten by the demons that abducted them at this very moment."
"...Sonozaki-san!"
"Aah, sorry.@k@vS10/43/410400160. We still don't know that for certain."
I could hear the anger in his voice from Sonozaki-san's imprudent choice of words.
Then Sonozaki-san shrugged her shoulders and casually rifled through a cabinet.
"No way... I never would've imagined anything like this..."
"It's like they were doing it as a warning to others."
"A warning?"
"Right.@k@vS10/41/410200148. Listen to me, or suffer a horrible death..."

"Yeah. As a matter of fact, that's exactly it.@k@|@y@vS10/62/410100568. There are specific rules the people of Hinamizawa are expected to follow.@k@vS10/62/410100569. And the villagers who violated those rules were supposedly killed in a terrible manner to set an example."
"...What are those rules?"

"Well, they mainly exist to keep the village isolated from the outside world.@k@vS10/62/410100571. "Don't let others into the village," and, "Don't leave the village."@k@vS10/62/410100572. The rules prevent the flow of people in and out of Hinamizawa."
"If those are the rules...@k@vS10/41/410200151. then maybe they were established to protect the villagers with partial demon blood, like in the story you told us earlier...?"
"...That might be it."
Otobe quickly turned his head away as I agreed with his observation.
It was a bit surprising, because up until now I didn't think Otobe was a very reliable person.
"What... were you talking about earlier?"
"Oh, right... we had a conversation about the history of Hinamizawa while you were out, Miyuki-chan..."
"Was it the story... about the alien parasites?"
"Aliens?@k@vS10/41/410200153. What are you talking about?!"
Though Miyuki-chan said that with a jovial smile, Otobe responded in a hysterical tone.
...The tense atmosphere from a moment ago dispersed in an instant.
"...Was that a weird thing to say?"
"No... I've heard that story before.@k@vS10/62/410100576. That theory recently made the rounds on the internet..."
"What's this about aliens?"
"...They say that in ancient times, aliens came to Earth aboard a flying saucer that landed at Onigafuchi swamp."
"The aliens control the minds of humans using parasites, and they've been spreading the word of Oyashiro-sama in order to maintain their way of life."
Otobe stood there dumbfounded, with his mouth hanging wide open.
Well, anyone would react that way the moment they hear something about aliens.
"How do I put it, um...@k@vS10/41/410200157. You two really are occult writers, huh?"
"Hold on..."
"...Uh, I'm not one of those."
Miyuki-chan denied it with a bitter smile as I shot Otobe a scornful glare. The way Otobe said "really" didn't sound like a compliment.
Damn it. I know it's not unreasonable, but it's still frustrating.
"Look... it's not just crazy stories like that, you hear?@k@vS10/62/410100581. There are other examples... umm..."
Then I pulled out a scrapbook and flipped through it to find a more down-to-earth explanation for the theory.
The sound of turning pages echoed throughout the ritual storehouse.

"According to that theory,@k@vS10/40/410300414. the most powerful figures within the village were mind controlled by the aliens.@k@vS10/40/410300415. And they used them to bend the villagers to their will."
"I-I see..."
"I've seen people discussing that online... They also mentioned how members of the Sonozaki family tortured innocent people."
"Sonozaki family...?!@k@vS10/41/410200160. You mean... that Sonozaki-san's family?"
"Well... that's what they said, anyway."
Miyuki-chan broke into laughter halfway through saying that.
It was clear that she didn't take the story seriously.
But when standing among all these tools, it seemed misplaced to blame all these atrocious murders on the work of aliens... It wasn't a convincing story.
Even so, once Otobe heard the name "Sonozaki" he began frantically looking back and forth between Miyuki-chan and Sonozaki-san.
"Well, it's true that the Sonozaki family is one of the most powerful organizations in the village, that said..."
"No way...@k@vS10/41/410200162. You're saying that Sonozaki-san is being controlled by alien parasites?@k@vS10/41/410200163. That's absurd..."
Otobe was looking pale until a little while ago, but a smile finally started to form on his face. Perhaps he thought the story far too ridiculous to be believable.
"What's wrong, Otobe-kun?"
"Oh, no... It was just kind of funny to imagine Mion-san as an alien..."
"Mion...san...?"
"Right. Mion... Sonozaki-san."
Taken by surprise, I quickly flipped back through the scrapbook.
"Ryuunosuke-san...?"
"Aah, hold on.@k@vS10/62/410100586. No way... It can't be...?"
Mion Sonozaki...
That's it. ...I finally understood why that name stuck out to me.
And there was no mistaking it. I definitely saw that name in this scrapbook earlier.
My hands lightly trembled as I flipped through the pages.
Otobe apparently didn't notice my agitation. He seemed to be relaxed and enjoying our discussion of absurd theories.
"Anyway, this stuff about aliens is a wild story.@k@vS10/41/410200167. I don't know who thought it up, but it's just so crazy..."

"But you know, there was someone out there who actually believed this story?@k@vS10/40/410300421. A girl from this village held a school hostage, doused a classroom in gasoline, and fought desperately to get more people to believe this alien theory."
"And her actions seem to lay the groundwork for the modern alien theory.@k@vS10/40/410300423. Right, Ryuunosuke-san?"
"R-right..."
I briefly looked up from the scrapbook to make it look like I was paying attention.
The police tried negotiating with her, but they failed. A massive explosion occurred within the school building, killing the culprit and all the students held hostages within.
And the very next day, the gas disaster occurred...
"By the way, there are rumors that this girl predicted the gas disaster as well."
"And there are still some doubts that the gas disaster was a natural disaster at all."
"Some of the more wild conspiracy theorists allege that a secret government organization released the gas in order to annihilate the aliens."
"Ahaha. When you go that far out, it's impossible to keep up..."
"Hehehe..."
Just then, Sonozaki-san let out a stifled laugh. She was apparently listening to our conversation while rummaging through the torture tools.
And then...
"...So, this alien parasite of yours...@k@vS10/43/410400163. probably possessed me too...?"
"W-what are you talking about?@k@vS10/41/410200170. Quit joking around..."
"Ahahahahahahaha! ...That's right!@k@vS10/43/410400165. I'm not actually "me" at all.@k@vS10/43/410400166. The truth is...@k@vS10/43/410400167. I'm being possessed! Hehehe..."
Sonozaki-san's eyes glimmered in the dark as she let out a creepy laugh.
She had a mysterious, terrifying visage... and an eerie shadow was cast behind her back.
I felt an inhuman aura emanating from Sonozaki-san as she turned her back to the statue of Oyashiro-sama.
She looked like... a demon, herself.
Indeed. Juxtaposed with the fierce shadow of Oyashiro-sama... she looked inhuman.
"P-please, stop making these weird jokes..."
Otobe trembled as he exclaimed that with all his might. I, on the other hand... was too embarrassed to say anything at all.
*rustle*
I took a step back and accidentally bumped my arm into something, making me drop my scrapbook.
"Ryuunosuke-san..."
"Aah, sorry."
I bent down to pick up the scrapbook... but my hand suddenly stopped.
...What I found down there was...
The article I had been searching for.
And the name Mion Sonozaki was clearly written there.
"Hey, take a look at this..."
"Ryuunosuke-san? What is it?"
"Miyuki-chan... come here for a second."
I took a quick glance at Sonozaki-san, then grabbed Miyuki-chan by the arm.
"Ryuunosuke-san...?"
"Look, just come with me..."
I focused my gaze downward to avoid making eye contact with Sonozaki-san as I dragged Miyuki-chan out of the ritual storehouse.
"Huh...?!@k@vS10/41/410200174. Arakawa-san? Sorimachi-san...?"
Feeling left behind, Otobe began frantically looking around.
Then, Sonozaki-san resumed hunting through the torture tools inside the ritual storehouse.
"...I've found it.@k@vS10/43/410400169. The thing I was looking for... This scroll."
Sonozaki-san smiled to herself as she pushed the pile of tools aside to reach for the scroll. When Otobe saw that, he caught his breath and swallowed hard.
Behind the scroll, the figure of a long-haired girl was reflected...
"Rika-chan... thank you..."
Then, the girl muttered something to Sonozaki-san with a thin smile on her face, before vanishing into the air...!
"With this... everything is complete..."
The statue of Oyashiro-sama was standing behind Sonozaki-san, with a sickle in hand.
In Otobe's mind, that sight overlapped with a vision of a demon slashing a human open in devotion to Oyashiro-sama.
"......@k@vS10/41/410200177. A... Arakawa-san! ...Sorimachi-san!!"
Otobe was short of breath as he came running out of the ritual storehouse.
"Miyuki-chan, take look at this..."
Outside the ritual storehouse, I spread open the scrapbook and held it up to Miyuki-chan.
It was a newspaper article from 1983, listing the victims from the school hostage incident.
"A list of victims...?@k@vS10/40/410300432. What about it?"
"Right here. Take a look...!"
I pointed at the list of victims from the gasoline explosion at the school.
Satoko Houjou, Rika Furude, Mion Sonozaki, Rena...
Miyuki-chan's eyes fixated on a single entry.
...Mion Sonozaki.
"That's right. Mion Sonozaki."
"I find it hard to believe, too.@k@vS10/62/410100602. But..."
Miyuki-chan kept silent and continued gazing at the scrapbook.
"Haah... haah...@k@vS10/41/410200179. Arakawa-saan! Sorimachi-saan!"
Otobe ran over, then started to look perplexed. Maybe he noticed the tense atmosphere around Miyuki-chan and me.
"...What's wrong?"
"She's dead."
"Huh?"
I was frustrated by the look of absolute incomprehension on Otobe's face.
"Because... Mion Sonozaki died over 20 years ago!"
"Take a look!"
I handed the scrapbook to Otobe and pointed to the article.
Otobe carefully looked over the page I indicated, and eventually...
*thud*
He dropped the scrapbook. ...And Miyuki-chan stared blankly at the ritual storehouse, making no effort to hide her bewilderment.
"What... is a dead person doing here...?"
"It can't be true... That's impossible...@k@vS10/40/410300438. If that's the case... then who is that person over there...?"
A deceased person from this very village, guiding living beings into the world of the dead...
The words from my father's diary ran through my head over and over again.
No way... the dead are reaching out to the living...?
"This village... has something strange going on... something that defies common sense..."
I tried to spin a few words, but nothing I said brought me any peace of mind.
I've investigated many places as an occult writer, but this was a first for me.
A village that's been blockaded for over 20 years, where the dead walk freely and the living can be "demoned away" in an instant...
"What... What the hell is going on here...?"
Maybe I shouldn't have come to Hinamizawa after all...
I knew nothing of the incomprehensible power at work within this village.
